questions.q: What is the primary benefit of implementing sleep modes in a microcontroller-based system?
options: Sleep modes increase the CPU clock frequency
Sleep modes reduce power consumption by disabling unused modules and lowering clock frequency or stopping the CPU
Sleep modes allow the processor to execute more instructions per cycle
Sleep modes improve data storage capacity
questions.explanation: Sleep modes disable peripheral clocks, lower system frequency, or halt the CPU while keeping memory intact. This dramatically reduces current draw (often 100x lower). The CPU can wake on specific interrupts. This is crucial for battery-powered devices, extending runtime from days to years.

questions.q: In low-power design, what is 'dynamic power consumption' primarily affected by?
options: The amount of installed RAM
The CPU frequency and supply voltage (P = CV²f)
The color of the PCB
The geographical location of the device
questions.explanation: Dynamic power (switching power) follows P ∝ CV²f, where C is capacitance, V is voltage, and f is frequency. Reducing frequency by half cuts dynamic power roughly in half; reducing voltage by half cuts it by a quarter. Static (leakage) power also exists but depends primarily on technology and temperature.

questions.q: What is duty cycling in the context of low-power embedded systems?
options: Regularly replacing batteries on a fixed schedule
Periodically activating the system for brief work periods then returning to sleep, reducing average power consumption
Reducing the supply voltage continuously throughout the day
Running the CPU at maximum speed for short bursts
questions.explanation: Duty cycling alternates between active (working) and sleep states. For example, a sensor that wakes every 10 seconds, reads for 100ms, then sleeps for 9.9 seconds has a ~1% duty cycle. Average power is (active power × duty cycle) + (sleep power × (1 - duty cycle)), often achieving dramatic reductions.

questions.q: Which component typically consumes the most power in a wireless IoT device?
options: The microprocessor executing instructions
The wireless radio transmitting and receiving data
The LED indicators
The crystal oscillator providing clock signals
questions.explanation: Wireless transceivers (Wi-Fi, cellular, Bluetooth) consume far more power than computation. A radio transmission can draw 100+ mA, while the CPU in sleep mode draws microamps. This is why IoT devices prioritize reducing radio on-time through efficient communication and batching data.

questions.q: What is 'leakage current' in modern microcontroller design, and why does it matter at very low power consumption?
options: Current that 'leaks' out of the battery into the environment
Static current drawn by transistors even when not switching, becoming significant in sleep mode when dynamic power is negligible
Current that flows through the battery's internal resistance
The current used to charge capacitors during startup
questions.explanation: Leakage current (from reverse bias and subthreshold conduction in transistors) is typically negligible during high-speed operation but dominates in deep sleep modes. In modern sub-micron processes, leakage is substantial, limiting minimum achievable power. This is why specialized ultra-low-power MCUs use special design techniques.

questions.q: How does voltage scaling (also called DVFS - Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling) reduce power consumption?
options: It temporarily disables the power supply to unused modules
It reduces both operating voltage and frequency together, cutting dynamic power quadratically with voltage and linearly with frequency
It increases voltage to reduce current draw
It has no real effect on power consumption
questions.explanation: DVFS lowers voltage proportionally with frequency, so P = CV²f decreases as V² (squared) with voltage reduction plus linear reduction from lower f. For example, halving both voltage and frequency reduces power by ~75%. The trade-off is longer execution time; tasks must complete within their deadline.

questions.q: In battery-powered embedded systems, why is the battery's self-discharge rate important for ultra-long-lived designs?
options: It has no impact on system lifetime
In devices sleeping for years (like remote sensors), battery self-discharge can exceed microcontroller leakage current, limiting achievable lifetime regardless of low-power techniques
Battery self-discharge occurs only during active operation
Self-discharge is prevented by inserting a diode
questions.explanation: Even the best low-power MCUs in deep sleep draw only microamps. However, alkaline batteries self-discharge at similar or higher rates (microamp range). For 10-year lifetimes, self-discharge can dominate power budget, requiring careful battery selection or harvesting techniques to extend life.

questions.q: What is energy harvesting in the context of low-power embedded systems?
options: Collecting electricity from a power outlet efficiently
Extracting energy from the environment (solar, thermal, vibration) to reduce or eliminate battery dependence
Recharging batteries quickly
Storing energy in capacitors instead of batteries
questions.explanation: Energy harvesting scavenges power from ambient sources: photovoltaic (solar), thermoelectric (temperature difference), piezoelectric (vibration), or RF (ambient radio). While individual sources provide limited power (μW to mW range), they can sustain maintenance-free operation for decades, eliminating battery replacement.

questions.q: Why is reducing the CPU clock frequency by using a lower-speed oscillator effective for extending battery life?
options: Slower clocks produce weaker signals that consume less power
Lower frequency reduces both dynamic power (P ∝ f) and allows lower voltage, compounding savings; tasks take longer but consume less total energy
Lower frequency completely eliminates leakage current
Slower clocks improve battery lifespan through chemical effects
questions.explanation: Lower frequency directly scales dynamic power (P ∝ f) and enables voltage reduction. While a task takes longer at lower frequency, the reduced instantaneous power and lower voltage (due to reduced margins) often result in net energy savings. This is optimal when deadlines aren't tight.

questions.q: In a low-power design using interrupt-driven wake-ups, why is minimizing the time spent awake critical?
options: Wake-up logic consumes no power
The brief active periods (transmitting, computing) consume far more power than sleep; reducing wake duration from 100ms to 10ms by 10x reduces average power by ~10x if duty cycle is small
CPU instructions inherently waste power
Wake-up is irrelevant to overall power budget
questions.explanation: In duty-cycle operation, most time is asleep (low power). The short active bursts dominate energy consumption due to much higher current draw. If duty cycle is 1% and active current is 50 mA (vs 10 μA asleep), the 1% contributes 83% of total energy. Shortening active time is the highest-impact optimization.
